data.title: Patient Opportunity Equity Strategy Q3 2025 Portfolio Activity And Attribution
data.description:
By Christina Siegel Malbon

Fund Highlights

During the third quarter of 2025, the Patient Opportunity Equity Strategy generated a total return of 14.1% net of fees. In comparison, the strategy’s unmanaged benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, returned 8.1%.

Using a three-factor performance attribution model, selection and interaction effects contributed to the portfolio’s outperformance which was partially offset by allocation effects. Precigen Convertible Perpetual Preferreds, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Precigen Warrants, Alibaba Group Holdings (BABA) and Precigen Inc. (PGEN) were the largest contributors to performance, while Dave & Buster’s Entertainment (PLAY), QXO Inc. (QXO), Crocs Inc. (CROX), IAC Inc. (IAC) and Mattel Inc. (MAT) were the largest detractors.

Relative to the index, the strategy was overweight the Consumer Discretionary, Communication Services, Financials, Energy, Industrials, and Health Care sectors on average during the quarter. With zero allocation to Real Estate, Utilities, Materials, and Consumer Staples, the portfolio was underweight these sectors along with Information Technology.

The portfolio eliminated five positions, Canada Goose Holdings Inc. (GOOS), Clear Secure Inc. (YOU), Kosmos Energy ltd (KOS), Precigen Convertible Perpetual Preferreds (which were converted into common), and Costco Wholesale Corp. Put Options (COST 1/16/26 P965) during the quarter.

The portfolio ended the quarter with 36 holdings, where the top 10 stocks represented 51.1% of total assets compared to 38.9% for the index, highlighting the portfolio’s meaningful active share of around 92.5%.

Portfolio Review

The market continued its upward trajectory in the third quarter, benefiting from resilient consumer spending, Federal Reserve rate cuts, and sustained AI investments. Our portfolio benefited as long-held positions broke through multi-year resistance overhangs.

As long-term investors, we estimate a company's value five, ten, and occasionally even twenty years into the future. Our goal is to identify businesses where underlying fundamentals diverge meaningfully from market expectations. Our favorite opportunities tend to have durable competitive advantages, large addressable markets, and world-class management teams. Companies with these attributes rarely trade at significant discounts, as their superior attributes are well recognized. However, periods of negative sentiment typically provide our entry point. Years of studying these businesses, understanding their management teams, and knowing how they create value gives us the conviction to build or add to positions during sell-offs.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) and Alibaba Group Holdings (BABA), positions we have held since 2020 and 2019, respectively, led portfolio performance after years of being dismissed as “un-investable”. Alphabet gained regulatory clarity while Alibaba unveiled aggressive AI investment plans. Although sentiment reversals are inherently unpredictable, our focus remains on the divergence between fundamentals and market expectations. In both cases, expectations had stayed low even as fundamentals strengthened, creating value we were willing to wait to realize.

We continue to monetize volatility in both directions. Weakness in travel-related names during the second quarter allowed us to meaningfully increase our positions in Delta Air Lines (DAL) and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH), both of which delivered strong returns in the third quarter. Similarly, we doubled our exposure to UnitedHealth Group (UNH) during this quarter’s pullback, establishing an attractive foundation for long-term returns.

Finally, Precigen (PGEN), the strategy’s top contributor, represents a relationship spanning more than a decade. As you may recall, we led a PIPE (private investment in public entity) transaction that gave us exposure to the company through convertible perpetual preferreds, and warrants. Our thesis was straightforward: the company possessed a valuable near-term asset (Papzimeos) and promising pipeline but needed capital to reach critical milestones. In early August, the FDA approved Papzimeos with a better than anticipated label. Following the ensuing rally, we converted our preferreds into common stock, realizing substantial gains while maintaining significant exposure.

The opportunity remains compelling. Papzimeos is now launching, while earlier-stage pipeline assets continue advancing. CEO Dr. Helen Sabzevari is an outstanding leader with a proven track record of bringing drugs to market. While the risks inherent in drug development remain high, we believe the risk/reward profile remains attractive, with the market still undervaluing the full potential of Papzimeos and the broader pipeline.

New and Eliminated

This quarter we exited five positions in the period, using the proceeds to reallocate capital towards our highest-conviction names. We collected tax losses by exiting Canada Goose Holdings Inc. (GOOS) and Kosmos Energy Ltd (KOS) in the quarter. We converted our Precigen Convertible Perpetual Preferreds into common stock in the period to take advantage of the conversion price. We closed our Costco Wholesale Corp. Put Option (COST 1/16/26 P965) while exiting our small position in Clear Secure Inc. (CLEAR).

*Contribution illustrated above are provided net of fees and includes cash.

Top Contributors

Precigen Inc. (PGEN) was a top contributor to the portfolio following the early approval of Papziemos for the treatment of recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP) in early August. With this approval, Papziemos became the first-in-class "off-the-shelf" immunotherapy for a patient population with no other treatment options. As the company moves quickly to launch the drug, it inches closer to self-funding allowing it to advance its other pipeline assets toward approval. The company is led by Dr. Helen Sabzevari, who brings extensive expertise in immunotherapy-based therapeutics, having founded and served as Chief Scientific Officer of Compass Therapeutics. She has driven remarkable clinical progress at Precigen over the past few years, and we expect her to apply a similar playbook to their other pipeline assets in Phase 2 and Phase 1/1b development. While the stock has surged 194% year-to-date, we still see attractive upside given multiple blockbuster assets in development.

Alibaba Group Holdings (BABA) staged an impressive comeback in the third quarter, surging 58% as the company delivered results that exceeded cautious investor expectations. Their cloud business was the standout performer, posting 26% year-over-year growth while AI-related product revenue maintained triple-digit expansion for the eighth consecutive quarter. The company has signaled its AI ambitions by committing over $50 billion in investments over the next three years. Their latest model, Qwen-3-Max, has reportedly achieved overall performance surpassing both GPT-5 and Claude Opus 4. From a fundamental perspective, we continue to see an attractive setup. Alibaba is capitalizing on accelerating AI initiatives, renewed momentum in its Tmall platform, and rapid growth in instant shopping and local services. Despite substantial investment commitments, the company continues returning capital to shareholders through a 1% dividend yield and a robust buyback program. With Jack Ma returning to the company in an informal capacity, we believe Alibaba is well-positioned to lead China's AI race while maintaining lead market share in e-commerce.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) had a strong third quarter, climbing 38% over the period. The company continues to execute across its portfolio, with Waymo capturing significant market share and expanding into new cities, Gemini remaining a top performing AI model and improving search functionality, YouTube continuing to dominate user time spent and the company receiving a near best case outcome in the Search anti-trust case. Despite the strong move we believe the company remains significantly undervalued relative to its intrinsic value when considering all its market leading assets. While the company trades at 23x 2026 earnings, if you remove the money-losing and under-earning businesses, you are paying below a market multiple for the core Google business. We do not believe there are many other AI winners trading at such an attractive multiple.

Top Detractors

Dave & Buster's Entertainment Inc. (PLAY) was a top detractor in the quarter, giving back all the gains from the second quarter. The company is in the midst of a multi-year transformation that, following disappointing 2024 results, led to the abrupt departure of then-CEO Chris Morris. As the Chairman stepped in as interim CEO and refocused the company on a back-to-basics strategy, early green shoots emerged with improving same-store sales that drove the stock higher in the second quarter. However, improvement is rarely linear, and the company posted weaker trends in the third quarter leading to the sell-off. Despite the pullback, we continue to have conviction in the path forward. In July, the company announced Tarun Lal as the new CEO, a 25+ year veteran of Yum! Brands. While he hasn't provided a specific timeline, Lal expects to return the company to $675 million in EBITDA. At current valuations, this would represent a 5.6x multiple compared to the company's historical average of 13.4x. We believe that as the turnaround takes hold under experienced leadership, the valuation gap will close overtime.

QXO Inc. (QXO) gave up some ground in the third quarter as the market grew increasingly concerned about weakening construction activity. Despite potential macro headwinds, the company continues executing on its plan to integrate and optimize Beacon Roofing, which it acquired in April. Beacon was the first of what is expected to be a series of acquisition, as the company pursues a roll-up strategy in the highly fragmented building products distribution industry. QXO is leveraging a proven playbook that is management team has successfully executed across other sectors. With a strong track record and investor confidence, the company benefits from the ability to raise capital at attractive terms, giving it a competitive edge vs peers. Furthermore, management has proven their price discipline walking away from a bidding war for GMS Inc., which was ultimately acquired by Home Depot. We view this disciplined approach as a testament to management’s long-term focus. Over the next decade, QXO is targeting more than $50B in annual revenue. We have high conviction in Brad Jacobs’ leadership and believe the company is well positioned to become a long-term compounder.

Crocs Inc. (CROX) declined in the quarter following disappointing guidance. The company is facing pressure from wholesalers pulling back on orders and its own reduction in direct-to-consumer promotions, while HEYDUDE continues to underperform. The company has been working to turn around the HEYDUDE brand, which it acquired in 2022, but the brand continues to struggle with market saturation and limited awareness. Earlier this year, Terence Reilly joined as EVP and President of HEYDUDE. Reilly is best known for his transformative work at Stanley, where he reshaped the insulated drinkware brand into a cultural phenomenon. The company is hoping he can bring similar success to HEYDUDE. Despite near-term headwinds, the long-term setup remains favorable. In the meantime, the company generates substantial cash flow and continues returning capital to shareholders through an outstanding buyback program representing 25.6% of shares outstanding.

*Attribution illustrated above are provided Quarter-to-Date, net of fees, and includes cash.

Market Proxy is S&P 500. Returns greater than 1 year are annualized. Source: Bloomberg and Patient Capital Management

The data provided is from APX and Patient Capital Management, LLC and is believed to be reliable, but is not guaranteed as to its timeliness or accuracy. Percentages and returns may not sum to 100% due to rounding effects. A three-factor attribution consists of the allocation effect, selection effect, and the interaction effect, which sum to the portfolio's performance relative to the benchmark.

• Allocation. The allocation effect represents the portion of the portfolio's excess return attributable to differences in sector weights between the portfolio and the benchmark index.

• Selection. The selection effect represents the portion of the portfolio's excess return attributable to differences in the weights of individual securities within each sector between the portfolio and the benchmark index.

• Interaction. Most complex and sometimes counterintuitive, the interaction effect represents the portion of the portfolio’s excess return attributable to combining sector allocation decisions with security selection decisions, and is often thought of as measuring the accuracy of manager’s convictions.

Please note that the methodology used by our independent third-party attribution software vendor will at times present sector allocation effects that are counterintuitive. For example, the software may calculate a negative sector effect even when the portfolio, on a weighted average basis for the period, was overweight an outperforming sector. Under the vendor's methodology, allocation effects in recent months may overwhelm the allocation effects from earlier in the period, particularly over longer time frames.

Returns illustrated above are provided net of fees and include cash. Total portfolio return figures provided above reflect the sum of the returns of the holdings in the representative account portfolio due to price movements and dividend payments or other sources of income.

data.title: Why Tesla's Stock Could Go Much Higher
data.description:
I recently upgraded Tesla Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) from a buy to a strong buy due to several factors, including CEO Elon Musk's enormous conviction in the company based on his recent trillion-dollar compensation plan, open market stock purchases of over $1 billion, and the company's transformative growth potential in AI, full self-driving ("FSD"), energy generation and storage, robotics, and more.

Tesla 1-Year Chart

Tesla's stock has also performed very well from a technical perspective recently. We witnessed a breakout above $380-400 resistance, which is around the price point where Elon accumulated a considerable number of shares.

While the stock put in a near-term high around $470-480 resistance, which is right around its ATH achieved toward the end of last year, it has had a very constructive pullback since.

Last Friday, as the U.S./China trade dispute escalated, Tesla hit a low of around $400 in after-hours trade. In full disclosure, I added about 2.5% (of portfolio holdings) slightly above the $400 level, bringing my total Tesla position to roughly 14% of my total portfolio assets now.

Technically speaking, the drop from $470 to $400 represented a correction of about 15%. Now, is this enough given the enormous run-up recently? And the swift bounce back following the dip? Will there be more downside ahead? Remains the million-dollar question. In my view, Tesla could temporarily dip below $400 on a transitory move lower, especially if the broader market has a more considerable pullback of 5-10% in future weeks.

If Tesla's stock falls below $400 support, my plan is to purchase another 3% around $380-370 and to build Tesla up to a full position with another 3% purchase around the critical $360-350 lower-end support zone.

Why I Would Like My Tesla Position Around 20%

My portfolio has often had a leading position. A long time ago, in about 2006, it was Apple (AAPL), and then in around 2011, it was Netflix (NFLX). Tesla has also been my top position in the past. More recently, I built Palantir (PLTR) up into a 15-20% position, and I even called it my top stock for the next decade when its price was still only around 8 bucks.

Now, due to Tesla's enormous growth potential in transformative and disruptive industries like AI, EVs, FSD, robotics, energy generation and storage, and more, Tesla's stock could go considerably higher in future years.

Moreover, it's not only that Tesla can lead in many of these highly lucrative futuristic segments, but Tesla could potentially dominate segments like robotics, FSD, energy generation and storage, and potentially even EVs again.

Tesla's enormous and highly efficient ecosystem is one of its most powerful moats and competitive advantages. Additionally, Tesla's remarkable economies of scale capabilities also provide it with a significant advantage over its adversaries.

In addition to Tesla's enormous ecosystem and highly advanced infrastructure, Elon's other companies like SpaceX (SPACE), Neuralink, xAI, the Boring Company, and other ventures under his umbrella, may collaborate with Tesla, enabling it to gain an even greater edge over the competition moving forward.

Tesla's EV Business Is Breaking Records Again

It appears like Tesla's EV business is back. Let's look at Tesla's latest delivery numbers, as Tesla delivered a record number of vehicles in the third quarter.

Tesla delivered about 497.1K vehicles in Q3 alone, marking its best quarter delivery-wise ever. This dynamic illustrates about an 8% increase over last year's roughly 462K delivery report.

We can also presume that due to the Model Y refresh and other upgrades, Tesla's average selling prices "ASPs" likely increased in Q3. Due to the new Model Ys, general price increases, and higher-end Model 3 sales, the Model 3/Y segment may have generated an ASP of roughly $43K per vehicle in Q3.

Therefore, the Model 3/Y segment alone may have generated about $20.27 billion in revenues (adjusted for lease accounting) in Q3. The ASP in the premium Model S/X segment may have remained around $100K, which would equate to roughly $1.48B in sales.

Based on previous reports and all available public information, I estimate that Tesla's leasing revenues were around $450 million in Q3. Automotive credit revenues may have been about $500 million. Energy generation and storage could have been about $3 billion, and Tesla's services and "other" segment may have provided about $3.3 billion in sales last quarter.

My Q3 Sales Projections

  • Model 3/Y Segment: $20.27B
  • Other Model Segment: $1.48B
  • Leasing: $450M
  • Regulatory Credits: $500M.

Total Automotive Revenues: $22.7B

  • Energy Generation and Storage: $3B
  • Services and Other: $3.3B.

Total Q3 Sales: $29B

The consensus estimate now is only for about $26.46B in sales, which is considerably lower than my figure. However, some high-end estimates go up to about $29B.

Therefore, I believe some analysts may be behind the curve on Tesla and could be surprised by higher-than-anticipated sales and profitability when Tesla reports Q3 earnings on Wednesday next week.

However, let's take things a bit further and see how profitable Q3 may be for the company. Last quarter, Tesla delivered a very healthy 17.2% gross margin. However, given the higher ASPs and potential continued improvements in production efficiencies, Tesla's gross margin could come in at about 18% for Q3.

  • Gross Margin: 18%.

Gross Profit: $5.22B

This dynamic implies that Tesla's gross profit may be about $5.22B for Q3, considerably higher than last quarter's $3.88B gross profit. Tesla may have improved its SG&A expenses over the last quarter, but for the sake of being conservative, let's presume that they remain about the same.

  • R&D Costs: $1.55B
  • SG&A Expenses: $1.45B
  • Operating Costs: $2B.

Operating Income: $3.22B

Operating Margin: 11.1%

  • Interest/expense/other: $300M
  • Pre-Tax Income: $2.92B.

Net Income $2.48B

EPS (normalized): $0.70

Why Analysts May Be Too Pessimistic

The consensus EPS estimate for Q3 is only $0.55. Lower-end estimates drift down to only $0.33. However, my estimate is around the higher end of the range, which is roughly $0.70. I believe Tesla can achieve this figure, which would be very bullish for Tesla's sentiment and its stock price.

Therefore, Tesla's stock could experience a considerable move, possibly to new ATHs after earnings, and it may continue trending higher after that. Future growth drivers like FSD, AI, robotics, energy, and other segments should enable substantial sales and earnings growth to materialize, which should also enable Tesla to continue to trade at a premium multiple.

Due to this bullish intermediate and long-term dynamic, I have a $650 12-month price target on Tesla's stock. My longer-term three-to-six-year price target range is $2,500-3,500 for Tesla.

Tesla Risks

Of course, Tesla has risks despite my bullish thesis. There are execution risks, competition concerns, doubts about Elon's competency, and his involvement with politics. There are also risks to future EV demand, FSD adoption, AI monetization, robotics, and other factors that may need more time to play out in order to show true profitability potential down the line. Tesla's valuation may be high by some metrics, and the company may be arguably overvalued. Therefore, investors should consider these and other risks before investing in Tesla's stock.

data.title: JD.com Seems To Be In Trouble, But Here's Why I'm Still Buying
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Kaavish Capital as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

JD.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:JD) had a mixed Q2 2025 with robust top line growth but sharply weaker profitability. Total revenues rose 22.4% YoY, fastest growth since Q4 2021. JD Retail was up 20.6% and achieved a record 4.5% operating margin helped by the government’s trade-in program and the 618 shopping festival, which generated RMB ¥855.6 billion in GMV and over 2.2 billion orders. However, net income fell 51% YoY to RMB ¥6.2 billion as heavy subsidies and marketing for its fast-growing but fiercely competitive food delivery unit compressed margins to just 0.3%. To understand these results and especially JD’s decision to invest heavily in food delivery, we need to understand the changes taking place in the largest retail market in the world.

Retail In China Is In Flux And JD Is At A Disadvantage

Retail in China is going through a seismic shift with the next stage in its evolution being integrated consumption platforms. Meituan (OTCPK: OTCPK:MPNGF) is the pioneer in this space. It started as a food delivery platform but then expanded into instant retail, dining & entertainment, travel & mobility, home & local services and financial services. It attracted hostile attention when its instant retail business found success in categories traditionally dominated by JD and Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) such as electronics and personal care. Since then the two e-commerce giants have responded. Alibaba consolidated its existing food delivery business Ele.me (privately held) and travel business Fliggy (privately held) into its China e-commerce division and launched Taobao Flash Sale, a revamp of the its “Hourly Delivery” service offering food delivery and instant retail, that features prominently on Taobao (privately held). JD in turn announced entering a host of verticals including food delivery, hospitality, and local services.

The consensus view is that food delivery is the foundation that allows for instant retail and ultimately integrated consumption. It has the highest ordering frequency across retail, which allows platforms to engage armies of riders delivering millions of orders per day and to optimize dispatching, routing and batching algorithms necessary for achieving positive unit economics. It is therefore not surprising that food delivery has become the main battleground and JD is obviously at a disadvantage here with both Meituan and Alibaba, through Ele.me, having established food delivery businesses. JD is also not the first major player to covet a piece of the booming delivery market, Baidu, Inc. (BIDU), Didi Global Inc. (OTCPK:DIDIY) and ByteDance (privately held) have all tried and failed to get a foothold. However, JD already has relevant experience in instant retail thorough its JD Fresh and JD NOW services, access to a last-mile delivery network through its ownership of Dada Nexus (privately held) and transferable expertise from its core competency in supply chain and logistics.

Instant Retail Is Eroding JD’s Competitive Advantage

JD’s key differentiating features are speed and product authenticity. A decade ago when Chinese platforms were inundated with fakes, JD’s owned inventory model helped it become the platform of choice for high ticket items like electronics and home appliances. In addition, JD’s self-operated logistics network, offered and still offers the fastest delivery times for e-commerce orders. Instant retail does not have a product authenticity problem as products are delivered from brands’ own shops or forward warehouses stocked with products supplied by brands. On the speed front, JD’s same day or next day delivery now feels like dial-up internet in the Wi-Fi era of 30 minute delivery. The impact is already beginning to show.

Meituan launched its instant retail service named Meituan Flash Purchase back in 2018. Originally meant for everyday necessities and impulse buys, Meituan began to experiment with additional categories including consumer electronics and found success during the Covid lockdowns. The momentum continued to build even after lockdowns ended as consumers, especially younger ones, got used to 30 minute deliveries. For JD, 2024 was a wake-up call as it was the first time since at least 2018, when JD started reporting Electronics and Home Appliances figures, that it lost market share in those categories. It is because of this, JD chose to dive head first into the tempestuous waters of food delivery and spent an entire quarter’s earnings promoting it.

To make matters worse, Alibaba has upped the ante by integrating food delivery and instant retail into its broader ecosystem. It has made instant retail a top priority alongside AI and its beginning to show. After trailing Meituan for years, it has gone from having less than half the order volume to near equivalency in four months since the launch of Taobao Flash Sale in April. JD will find it difficult to compete with the scale, ecosystem and financial resources of Alibaba this time around as it does not have a competitive advantage within instant retail like it does in e-commerce.

JD’s Early Gains In Food Delivery Are Already Reversing

JD officially announced entry into the food delivery market on Feb 11, 2025 and started delivering orders from the beginning of March. Initial response was promising with number of daily orders hitting 5 million in mid-April and then quintupling to 25 million during the 618 festival as a result of a CNY ¥10 billion subsidy. As a reference, this is more than Ele.me average daily orders in 2024.

In July, Meituan and Alibaba responded with massive subsidies of their own. Alibaba committed to CNY ¥50 billion in subsidies over the next twelve months on Taobao Flash Sale and Meituan made a CNY ¥100 billion subsidy commitment over the next three years. The result was the delivery market size almost doubling within a matter of weeks and total number of daily orders almost tripling. The discrepancy in the growth rates is due to the massive increase in low value drinks orders since the launch of the subsidies. This makes sense as demand for meals is relatively inelastic since people might order takeout more often due to subsidies but they won’t start eating more meals in a day. In contrast, people are likely to increase both their ordering frequency and daily intake to benefit from drink subsidies.

JD’s market share peaked at around 25% during the 618 festival and has been declining since. JD announced a second CNY 10 billion program in July but this one was more focused on merchant support than consumer discounts. It seems like JD is unwilling to engage in a subsidy war with its two more established competitors.

So Why Am I Buying?

Before I answer this question, I want to go back to the consensus view about the future of retail I shared earlier i.e. integrated consumption is the next evolutionary stage of retail and usage frequency will determine the winners. Whereas I agree with the general sentiment, I am not convinced that instant retail or integrated consumption will be the end of e-commerce. Extrapolation bias is clearly at play here as people are extending short-term trends over the long-term. Reminds me of the time when e-commerce was supposed to be the end of brick-and-mortar. Just as brick-and-mortar retailers such as Walmart were able to not only fight back but thrive in the current omni-channel environment, I believe the best e-commerce players will be able to adapt to an integrated consumption future. In light of this, I will now go over the reasons I believe JD is worth buying.

JD Does Not Need To Dominate Food Delivery

JD’s entry into the food delivery business was a defensive move triggered by Meituan’s encroachment into JD’s backyard (Consumer Electronics and Home Appliances). According to data from QuestMobile, there is an overlap of 355 million among monthly active users of JD and Meituan. These users most likely use the Mietuan app much more frequently and will inevitably be tempted to buy something they normally buy on JD. To tackle this JD had to increase the usage frequency of its own app.

The objective for JD is not to become the platform of choice for food delivery rather it wants to mitigate the risk of losing users to instant retail offerings of delivery platforms. JD will be more than okay with not making any money or even losing money on food delivery if it can achieve a moderate level of success. An increase in usage frequency will help it recoup some of the losses through savings from reduced spending on traffic acquisition and additional revenue from cross-selling. Ironically JD is taking a page from the brick-and-mortar playbook by using food as a loss leader to drive traffic and fend off competition.

As JD is not seeking leadership in food delivery or instant retail, it does not need to beat or even match competitors on every single metric. If it can be in the same ballpark as them on key metrics without bleeding cash, it should be able to hold on to its core customers.

JD’s Food Felivery Strategy Is Sound

JD entered the food delivery market knowing that it was outmatched in terms of scale and spending power. It therefore focused on differentiation by vociferously taking on the major pain points within the food delivery ecosystem while simultaneously capitalizing on the zeitgeist in China around social welfare and sustainable development to build brand equity.

The most widely discussed issue in China, within the food delivery space, has been the exploitation of riders by the incumbent platforms. Riders are employed as freelancers, even if they work full-time for the platform (260 days a year), and therefore not eligible for any of the employment benefits. JD has already hired 150,000 full time riders and is providing them with what are known in China as the five insurances and one housing fund, a type of social security system. As a show of solidarity with the riders, Richard Liu (JD’s founder) delivered orders himself and dined with other riders during the initial days of the launch of JD’s food delivery business.

Another topic of contention within the industry has been the commission charged to merchants by the platforms. There had been numerous rumors about merchants paying as high as 30% commission however, Meituan has insisted that its commission is capped at 8%. Despite this, a general perception of exploitation persists. JD has committed to a flat 5% commission and offered zero commission for the rest of the year to restaurants that joined the platform before May 1, 2025.

Finally, JD is also addressing user complaints of unhygienic food sold by ghost kitchens with fake or misleading listings on incumbent platforms. JD announced that it will take several measures to guarantee food quality on its platform by ensuring that merchants on its delivery platform have a physical location, a valid business license and a Dianping (Chinese Yelp) score of more than 3.8. In addition, JD recently launched its first self-operated food kitchen with the aim of providing meals at costs similar to those of ghost kitchens without compromising on quality. It plans to set up 10,000 such kitchens primarily in areas where there is a lot of ghost kitchen activity.

JD’s strategy is clever for several reasons. First, as mentioned earlier, it plays on important social themes and positions JD as a responsible player against “exploitative” incumbents. Secondly, JD’s approach uses the incumbents’ scale against them as it would be cost prohibitive for them to match JD’s commitments. Take rider benefits for example, Meituan had 7.45 million riders in 2023 out of which around 0.82 million were working full time. The second number is probably closer to a million now given Meituan’s continued growth since then. Paying the five insurances and one housing fund for the full time riders alone would cost Meituan around an additional CNY 14 billion a year (over 40% of its 2024 net income). Finally, its social positioning allows it to avoid competing solely on metrics like speed, reliability or selection where it is currently lagging.

JD’s Market Share For Consumer Electronics And Home Appliances Should Stabilize

JD’s market share losses to instant retail in its core Home Appliances and Consumer Electronics categories are not irreversible. Firstly, it is too early to tell whether the declines are due to a definite shift in consumer behavior or just to the fleeting appeal of novelty and promotion. Long-term market share will be decided by structural factors that will differentiate JD and its competitors in terms of product, price and service.

In terms of products, both JD and instant retailers offer the same branded products when it comes to electronics. JD does have an small advantage in terms of differentiation. JD operates an asset heavy model where it takes ownership of the inventory and the associated risks. This gives it leverage with the manufacturers/brands who are willing to offer differentiated products for its platforms. A manufacturer/brand would be less likely to do the same for an asset light platform as it would have to assume the inventory risk for the product.

JD also has an advantage in terms of price as the supply chain for e-commerce is less costly compared to that of instant retail. The large out of city warehouses are cheaper to operate compared to the various in-city storage methods employed by instant retail. Also, the delivery cost itself is lower as there is more room for optimization when delivery time is not constrained to a very short interval. However, brands and platforms are currently subsidizing instant retail customers by absorbing most of the extra cost and only charging the latter a small delivery fee. If this turns out to be sustainable, JD’s cost advantage will not be meaningful.

Service is where things get tricky for JD as instant retail has a definite advantage in terms of speed. However we must remember that JD has its own instant retail business JD NOW and should be able to expand it to incorporate consumer electronics and home appliances. Once it does that, JD’s speed disadvantage should disappear. Also we should not discount the user mindshare JD enjoys when it comes to electronics, which was evident during the recent iPhone 17 launch when JD sold more iPhones in the first minute than it did during the first full day of launch last year. According to a survey, more than 80% of respondents said JD was their preferred platform for buying the new iPhone, because of its authenticity and official authorization (56% of respondents), its after sales services (45% of respondents) and favorable prices (44% of respondents).

JD Is Successfully Pursuing Several Other Growth Avenues

JD has been trying to diversify its product mix for a long time and these efforts are beginning to pay dividend. Recently growth in General Merchandize has kept pace with that in Home Appliance and Consumer Electronics without the benefit of generous government subsidies. Part of this is undoubtedly due to increased traffic on account of the aforementioned subsidies but JD’s successful forays into supermarket and fashion space have played a pivotal role in this. General Merchandize has grown double digits in the first half of this year for the first time since 2021.

One of JD’s competitive advantages is its operational efficiency resulting from it self-owned and managed logistics network. JD Property (privately held) is responsible for developing the logistics infrastructure and JD Logistics, Inc. (OTCPK:JDLGF) is responsible for operating it. Originally meant to support JD’s supply chain, both of them are now servicing external customers and represent another growth area for JD.

JD Logistics opened up to third parties in 2017. Within 4 years, revenue from external customers surpassed that from JD retail and their share continues to increase. JD Logistics has the highest growth rate over the last 3 years among the largest third-party-logistics providers in China. Lately JD Logistics has been focusing on international expansion with international express delivery service increasing from 30 countries in 2023 to over 80 countries in 2024. In addition, it also manages over a 100 overseas warehouses and plans to double this capacity within this year. Finally, JD has also launched its first last-mile delivery service outside of China named JoyExpress in Saudi Arabia.

JD Property hasn’t been slouching either. Apart from meeting JD Logistics’ growing needs, it has been developing and renting out logistics infrastructure to external parties in and outside of China. By the end of 2024, the company managed over 260 modern infrastructure assets in 9 countries, with a total GFA exceeding 26 million square meters and a total asset management scale exceeding RMB ¥110 billion. There is a long runway for growth for JD Property both within and outside of China.

Richard Liu’s Second Wind Might Reinvigorate JD

The last few years have been turbulent ones for JD. CEO and founder Richard Liu stepped down as CEO in April 2022, during the Chinese government’s crackdown on tech. His successor Xu Li left after a year citing personal reasons and was succeeded by Sandy Xu, the CFO at the time. Lacking operational experience in retail, Sandy requested Richard to become more actively involved in operations till she found her footing.

In a recent public appearance at JD headquarters, Richard labelled the last five years a period of decline for JD marked by lack of innovation, growth and progress. On a personal level, he found these years to be the most undistinguished and least valuable in his entrepreneurial career.

More importantly, he talked about the future and laid out his vision, connecting the recent seemingly frenetic moves in food delivery, hospitality and stablecoins along with planned forays into beauty services, medical aesthetics, hospitals and sidewalk businesses like massage parlors. Richard sees JD’s efficiency as its competitive advantage and believes JD can help businesses across the disparate verticals mentioned earlier improve their supply chains. Specifically, for food delivery he mentioned that JD is not looking to make money at the front end, rather wants to optimize restaurant supply chains for price and quality, while eking out a margin for itself.

Richard also spoke about how international expansion is an important growth avenue for JD. He mentioned that he hopes to soon handover the domestic business entirely to Sandy and focus full time on the international business with Europe as the main focus. He talked about how his international e-commerce strategy is local e-commerce, where the business is managed by a local team and where most of the products are shipped locally. He also said that JD had spent the last three years building out the necessary infrastructure in Europe to make that happen. This is in sharp contrast to the business model of Temu and AliExpress and undoubtedly takes into account criticism the latter two have faced in foreign markets. The model is closer to how Amazon.com inc. (AMZN) operates and Richard admitted that without differentiation, JD will not have any advantage over Amazon. According to him, the source of differentiation will be a 1000 Chinese brands that JD has already signed up to help them expand internationally, which do not have a presence on Amazon or in local retailers.

For the consumer-facing end of the European business, JD has agreed terms with Ceconomy AG (OTCPK: OTCPK:MTAGF) and the deal has very recently been approved by the regulators. Ceconomy owns MediaMarkt (privately held) and Saturn (privately held), which are among the largest online shops for electronic goods in Europe and have a network of around 1,000 stores across the continent. JD has also rebranded its Ochama (privately held) business in Europe as Joybuy (privately held), though the reach of this business is very limited.

I do not assign a high probability of success to any of JD’s new initiatives. I see them as the price Richard Liu is willing to pay to bring about a culture change at JD. In recent years, JD has been so focused on protecting its core markets and hanging on to its core users that it became very reactive. It has been unsuccessfully chasing trailblazers in community buying, cross-border commerce and instant retail. Richard wants to change this dynamic by forcing the organization to come out of its comfort zone.

Valuation: The Market Is Already Pricing In A Dim Future

The foremost reason I am buying JD though is valuation. The market is pricing in significant business deterioration even when there is, as covered in detail above, evidence to the contrary. I’ll try to demonstrate how pessimistic the market is about JD by modeling a very conservative scenario.

Let's start with JD's established businesses and assume that recent growth, even in unrelated verticals, was solely due to the trade-in subsidies offered by the government for consumer electronics. Without these subsidies, growth in 2024 and 2025 would’ve been in line with that in 2023 (3.6%). Once the subsidies expire, JD’s electronics business will stop growing, effectively losing market share. In addition, blended operating margins excluding new initiatives, which had been improving even before the subsidies, will stall at the current levels.

To demonstrate how conservative the model is, here’s are comparison of recent JD growth rates across its verticals compared to our assumptions for the near future (2026 to 2028).

Now to add the impact of new initiatives, let’s keep it simple and assume complete failure. Let's assume losses from new initiatives will wipe out operating profits in 2025 and 2026. JD will start to divest new initiatives in 2027 and losses from new businesses will half. New businesses will be completely divested in 2028 and JD's operating profits will normalize.

To complete the model, we will assume net interest income remains constant at around CNY ¥6.5 billion and effective tax rate will be 15%. Working with these assumptions, we can model JD's P&L from 2025 to 2028 as shown in the table below.

For the DCF valuation, I like to use Warren Buffet's favorite Owner Earnings as these reflect sustainable cashflow to the owners. Using a 10% discount rate, and 0% terminal growth rate, and adding in net cash and marketable securities, we end up with a valuation of $52 billion or a ADR price of $34.84, which is very close to the current price. The reason I include marketable securities is that almost all of this amount is in term-deposits and held-to-maturity debt instruments, which do not fluctuate in value much. I did not include Investments in equity investors, which amount to $8 billion, in the valuation as there could be material changes to their value. However, it is unlikely they'll go to zero and offer a margin safety to investors.

Essentially, this means that even if JD's core businesses perform subpar and the new initiatives completely fail, investors won't lose much. Even with mediocre performance (3.6% short-term growth, stable margins, and 2% terminal growth), we end up with a valuation of $49.15 per ADR, an upside of around 36%. If JD can maintain its recent momentum and margins continue to improve, investors could double their money even if all new initiatives fail.

Risks To The Thesis

I will only talk about the operations specific risks here as macro issues such as US-China trade war and the state of the Chinese economy have been discussed ad nauseam on just about every investment forum.

My investment thesis rests on two key assumptions - JD's core business will survive the threat of instant retail and that JD will pursue the myriad new initiatives its announced in a manner that does not negatively effect the overall business. The following risks could undermine these assumptions.

If JD cannot shape its food delivery and JD NOW businesses into a competitive instant retail offering, it will lose customers who prioritize speed. This would be exacerbated if Alibaba and/or Meituan are able to reduce the gap in cost-efficiency and after sales service between instant retail and e-commerce before JD can get a foothold in the former.

Apart from being competitive, JD's instant retail service has to be financially viable. If JD cannot maintain user interest without subsidies and cannot improve delivery efficiency, operating margins will remain suppressed.

With management focus divided across a host of new initiatives, operational efficiency and service quality at the core could suffer, resulting in JD losing customers to competitors and lower profitability.

Finally, it is highly unlikely that JD will find success in all its new initiatives. If it is unable to cut its losses short and walk away from unsuccessful ventures, overall profitability will suffer.

Conclusion

The market is giving JD a melting ice-cube valuation mainly due to the threat of instant retail. In doing so, it is completely discounting JD’s ability to respond to the challenge. In addition, it is ignoring growth areas like General Merchandize and Logistics where JD has been gaining market share in recent years. Finally, it awards no value to a host of new initiatives launched by JD, including its renewed expansion outside of China. At this point, there is very little downside left and investors should profit handsomely if JD can just demonstrate business stability over the next few quarters.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch: Buy The Dip After Guidance Boost
data.description:
With the stock market looking increasingly jittery near all-time highs, my core strategy for managing my portfolio remains to reallocate the majority of my portfolio out of the expensive, large-cap tech plays that have dominated this year's gains while favoring more beaten-down small- and mid-cap stocks in other sectors that have their own growth catalysts to outperform the S&P 500.

Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF), in my view, is a stock that is getting increasingly more attractive as it falls. The apparel company has lost ~50% of its value this year, despite a recent guidance increase that puts the stock at a very cheap P/E multiple.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Abercrombie in June, when the stock was trading in the low $80s. Though this is a position that has required a frustrating amount of patience, I think the company's guidance boost, its preservation of margins amid the tariff environment, and continued growth of the Hollister brand are key drivers to push Abercrombie stock higher, not to mention an ultra-low valuation. Amid undue and unjustified pessimism for this stock, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Here is my updated long-term bull case for Abercrombie & Fitch:

  • Rebrand to basics appears to be driving positive comp sales growth for the company. Abercrombie has ditched its historic risqué image in favor of a more basic approach to young people's clothing, especially in jeans. This has yielded a successful return to positive comp sales for the company.
  • Hollister growth opportunity. The company's Hollister brand now generates more revenue than the eponymous Abercrombie brand and is still growing at a ~20% y/y clip. The success of Hollister may be due to its accessible price point, which is resonating with consumers in a budget-strapped environment.
  • A large buyback plan indicates management's confidence in undervaluation. As a clear signal that management believes the stock to be trading at a great price, Abercrombie & Fitch has an open, multi-year $1.3 billion share buyback program (roughly worth one-third of the company's current market cap), of which $1.1 billion remains unused. It plans to execute around ~$400 million in buybacks this year, which is amply supported by the ~$600 million of cash that the company has on its balance sheet (which is now unencumbered of debt).
  • Controlled tariff impact. The company has cited a 100 bps impact from tariffs on its imports from China and other markets, which is more than easily absorbed by the company's healthy gross margin profile.

Where we want to focus attention, however, is on the fact that Abercrombie actually boosted its full-year outlook, despite the downward motion in the stock over the past few months. Alongside Abercrombie's Q2 results, the company boosted its pro forma EPS expectations by 3% to $10.00-$10.50 (though this is still slightly below the original $10.40-$11.40 range that the company had before it cut its guidance in Q1). Abercrombie is now also expecting 5%-7% sales growth, versus 3%-6% previously.

At current share prices near $70, Abercrombie sits at a valuation of just 6.8x FY 2025 P/E (or just 5.6x FY 2025 P/E on an ex-cash basis, after considering the $603 million of cash on the company's latest balance sheet). This is an unbeatable valuation in today's market and a great way to diversify your portfolio away from expensive tech stocks.

Q2 download

Of course, there are some risks inherent in Abercrombie's financials, but that said, the stock's ultra-low valuation compensates for it. Let's now go through the company's latest Q2 results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Abercrombie's revenue grew 7% y/y to $2.31 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $2.30 billion (+6% y/y) by a one-point margin. Of course, the metric we're primarily concerned with is comparable sales, which, as a reminder, measures the revenue growth from existing store locations without including the impact of newly opened or newly closed stores. We note that Abercrombie's overall comp sales decelerated by one point to 3% y/y. This was driven by the Abercrombie brand's decline to -11% y/y, while Hollister still grew at a robust pace but decelerated four points y/y to 19% y/y, relative to 23% growth in Q1.

Cutting up the results by geography showcases an important story as well: we note that while the Americas have held up strong at 5% comps growth (accelerating one point relative to 4% growth in Q1), Europe decayed to a -5% y/y decline, representing 11 points of sequential deceleration for the company's second-largest market, which is just over 15% of revenue. Loss of customers in Europe is one of the core risks that Abercrombie faces, especially since Europe was driving double-digit growth for the company in FY 2024.

Speaking to the results by market on the Q2 earnings call, CEO Fran Horowitz noted as follows:

Regionally, the Americas achieved its 12th consecutive quarter of growth with net sales up 8% on continued traffic strength across direct channels. In EMEA, continued cross-channel growth in the U.K. was outweighed by softness in Germany and the remainder of European markets, with regional net sales lower by 1% against 16% growth in the second quarter of 2024. APAC continued to perform well, growing 12%, a nice cross-channel demand, while comparable sales grew 1% [...]

Looking to the second half of the year, we are increasing our full year 2025 net sales growth expectations based on our year-to-date results, supported by strong brand positioning, clean inventory, cross-channel traffic growth and our balance sheet.

We do note that Abercrombie is facing a tough retail environment in which many retail/apparel peers, such as Lululemon (LULU) and Gap (GAP), have reported much weaker or even negative comps, so relatively speaking, Abercrombie is holding up quite well, despite the sharp contraction in its P/E multiples.

On the profitability front, the company managed to offset a 3-point y/y decline in gross margins (driven by tariff costs, partially offset by favorable freight costs) with a 2-point efficiency in general and administrative costs. Operating income grew slightly y/y, while margins shed -80bps.

Key takeaways

Abercrombie & Fitch certainly isn't a squeaky-clean stock. We're mindful of continued contraction in the company's core Abercrombie brand, while sales trends in EMEA are also worrying. At the same time, however, Abercrombie is offsetting these headwinds successfully in building out the Hollister brand, while new top-tier partnerships, such as with the NFL, are boosting results in the Americas. Trading at such a low multiple of earnings for a company with zero debt is difficult to find in today's market. "Buy" here with confidence.

data.title: Patient Capital Management Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Are We There Yet?

Samantha McLemore

Short answer: Not yet—but we’re getting closer.Since March 2009, the S&P 500 has been a 10-bagger (~17% CAGR). Valuations are higher and speculation is louder, but institutions remain under-positioned, caution is pervasive, and the AI story is still unfolding—powerfully, but unevenly.Key Takeaways:

  • The real risk is over-caution. The past decade made this plain: HFRI ~4.3% vs. S&P 500 13.5% (2010 to 2019); endowments 6.8% vs. market 13.0% (2014 to 2024). Constant protection against drawdowns often sacrificed compounding.
  • Later innings ≠ last inning. We’re deep into a secular bull market that began at 12× forward earnings and now sits near ~23×. Sentiment remains skeptical (light institutional positioning; caution-heavy headlines), which is not typical of final peaks.
  • AI is transformative—and messy. Headlines warn of bubbles and capex risk; full interviews reveal nuanced optimism (Solomon, Bezos, Huang). Expect winners and losers, not a single narrative.
  • Category 1 vs. Category 2 errors. Investors obsess over being wrong (Type I) and underestimate the cost of inaction (Type II). Over time, missing big winners has been far costlier than enduring volatility.
  • Our stance. Patient, opportunistic, focused on avoiding permanent impairment, not volatility. We’ve owned what compounds (e.g., NVIDIA) and avoided capital-hungry “neo-clouds” where cash burn and valuation do not yet reconcile.

Why read on: to see how we square rising valuations with persistent skepticism, what we’re owning (and avoiding) in AI, and how we’re positioning for the end of the ride—whenever it arrives—without surrendering the power of compounding today.

Are We There Yet?

As a child, we rarely took vacations. Too expensive. A few times we drove 16 hours from Vermont to our grandparents’ house in South Carolina, a long and painful drive. We must have asked our parents a million times, “Are we there yet?” No wonder dad threatened to “cut a switch1.” Now I’m on the parent side of that classic question. Fortunately, air travel and Apple iPads have cut down the frequency!

The secular bull market that began in March 2009 has been characterized by a nearly constant fear that the end is nigh. “Are we there yet?”

The secular bull market gains have been stunning. In Peter Lynch parlance, from the 2009 lows to today, the S&P 500 produced a 10-bagger, compounding at 17.0% per year. It started with pure panic and depressed valuations. The S&P 500 traded at 12x forward 12-month earnings. Now, there’s significantly more optimism and valuations are almost double (23x forward 12-month earnings).

Unfortunately, most investors have failed to fully participate in the gains. Financial crisis trauma led to a costly, myopic focus on risk protection. Hedge funds, as measured by the HFRI Index, gained 4.3% per year on average in the 2010s, woefully lagging the S&P 500’s 13.5% annual return. A stark contrast to the outperformance they notched prior to the financial crisis.

To be sure, there are more signs of speculation now. AI deals announced for exorbitant sums with structures harkening back to disastrous Tech Bubble “vendor financing”. Young retail investors making bank on crypto and AI….YOLO.2 I even saw a recent X post claiming everyone in a room was trading on their brokerage sites.

Patient Capital has an annual employee stock picking contest where we pick 10 stocks for the year. Our non-investment team’s portfolios are up 41% on average for the year – nearly triple the S&P 500’s return. Every single administrative professional is beating the market. It’s not usually so easy to crush the market. We are in a bull market indeed.

Does that mean we’re “there”?

Institutional risk tolerance continues to be muted. University endowment returns have substantially lagged the market. Through the end of 2024, the NACUBO Commonfund Study of Endowments reported that endowments returned 6.8% per year over the preceding decade, a little over half the S&P 500’s 13.0% annual return.

Goldman Sachs (GS) recently reported light institutional fund positioning. Its sentiment indicator clocked its 30th consecutive negative reading, the third longest streak since 2015 (the two longer ones were both 32 weeks). Concerns, from tariffs and government shutdowns to AI capital spend and geopolitics, keep caution elevated.

At the Italian Tech Week in early October, Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon said, “I see complacency around risk-taking… I wouldn’t be surprised if in the next 12 to 24 months we see a drawdown.” He predicted there would be winners and losers.

At the same conference, Jeff Bezos called the AI boom, “an industrial bubble.” He said markets struggle to differentiate between winners and losers during excitement, citing the unusual circumstance of 6-person startups being funded at $20B valuations.

Those cautionary headlines spread rapidly.

If you listen to the entire interviews, they sounded far more positive than suggested. Solomon said he wants to spend much more on AI but is constrained by earnings considerations.

Bezos said it’s hard to fathom how transformative AI is. He said the horizontal enabling layer will improve quality and productivity for literally every company. He said it’s the most exciting time to be alive because of three concurrent Golden Ages: space travel, AI and robotics.

Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia (NVDA), recently spoke on the B-squared podcast with Brad Gerstner. Jensen said he’s not concerned about an overbuild of AI in the near-term. “Until we fully convert all general purpose computing to accelerated computing and AI…I think the chances [of a glut] are low.”

OpenAI (OPENAI) and AMD (AMD) announced another large deal, spurred by what OpenAI said is a shortage of compute.

We’ve compared this period to the late 90s or Nifty Fifty periods, both of which entailed spectacular gains and ended with secular bull market peaks. However, many people got defensive way too early and missed out. Greenspan’s famous “irrational exuberance” line in late 1996 was followed by 116% market gains! Even after the crash, the market didn’t fall to the levels where he uttered that line.

Paul Tudor Jones, one of the greatest traders of our times, recently told CNBC, “It’s like 1999...the ingredients are in place for some kind of blow off” top…“If anything, now is so much more potentially explosive than 1999. You need to position yourself like it's October 1999. The Nasdaq doubled from October 1999 to March 2000.”

Everyone seems to agree we are in a bubble. However, the magnitude, scope, and even existence of a bubble is only ever clear in hindsight. A few things seem clear at present:

  1. AI is transformative with broad usefulness and impact. It’s attracting significant attention and capital.
  2. It’s costly to build AI infrastructure, increasing the risk of subpar returns.
  3. Judging from valuations and sentiment, we are in the later stages of the secular bull market. Whether that occurs in months or many years, no one knows.
  4. Speculative activity is growing. Bezos cited startup funding. OpenAI’s recent $500B valuation is 25x its 2021 GPT-3 post releases $20B valuation. OpenAI is reported to have committed to orders for over $1T in infrastructure spend.
  5. Significant skepticism still exists. Cautionary headlines fill the news. Nvidia, arguably the main economic beneficiary of the boom, trades for 33x next 12 months earnings, a far cry from Cisco’s peak above 100x. Markets are complex, adaptive systems, and people have learned from past bubbles.

So where does that leave us? Howard Marks’ Jan 2025 piece, “On Bubble Watch,” brilliantly explained bubbles and summed up the pros and cons of the current environment well. The key point, “For me, it’s psychological extremeness that marks a bubble.”

Bubbles don’t typically peak with the world on high alert. At peaks, even the most bearish have usually thrown in the towel on their skepticism. But who knows? We approach markets with a huge dose of humility. There’s far more we don’t know than we know. Vigilance and agility are paramount.

Over the past decade and a half, people have cost themselves much more by being too cautious. People are much more sensitive to Category 1 investing errors (acting when you shouldn’t), than Category 2 ones (failing to act). See ChatGPT’s Summary below. Given losses are twice as painful as gains are pleasurable, investors aren’t as sensitive to missing money-making opportunities as they should be.

Those endowments that earned 6.8% per year over the past decade turned $10,000 into $19,307. If they’d invested in an S&P index fund instead, their 13.0% (or close to it with minor fees) returns would have resulted in $33,946 instead, 1.8x as much.

The Tech Bubble crash was one of the worst in history, entailing a 49% peak-to-trough decline. It was a secular market peak that coincided with a bubble.

If a comparable crash occurred today, the ~$34K index fund balance would fall to $17,312. Endowments should fare better. If their market capture rate is proportional (52.3% of the market’s upside and downside), their losses would be 25.6%, yielding a final balance of $14,358.

The index fund would still produce a balance 20% higher, despite being exposed to bigger losses. Markets rise over time, and compounding is powerful. You’re financially better off capturing the volatility on both sides. Psychologically, though, you’re exposed.

Market crashes like the Tech Bubble have happened only 5 times in the past century, a rate of roughly once every 20 years. The former analysis actually understates the benefits of downside volatility tolerance. Using a compounding period of 20 years before a crash, the index investor winds up with more than twice as much as the endowment investor (detailed in table).

We did suffer two whopper ~50% crashes within a decade in the 2000s. First, the Tech Bubble, then the Financial Crisis. This experience colored a generation of investors. It’s an important origin of the heightened risk awareness we see today.

My longtime colleague, Bill Miller, often pointed out that when people think of risk they only think of the risk of loss if things go wrong, but risk is two-sided and asymmetrical. You can only lose 100% of what you have at risk, but if things go right, you have the possibility of making many hundreds or even thousands of times what you are risking.

People only tend to think about this risk of missing out on gains during the later stages of a bull market. Does that mean people should get more aggressive today? Not at all.

A cardinal sin in investing is doing today what you should have done many years ago. Unfortunately, this happens all the time. It’s easy to see what you should have done differently. The incentive to do something to fix performance problems is significant.

With valuations near historical highs and pockets of euphoria, more caution is warranted. Long-term return prospects are lower than the past decade given the starting point.

Another great line from The Money Game states, “The only real protection against all the vagaries…is to have an identity so firm it is not influenced by all the brouhaha in the marketplace.” It goes on, “The end object of investment is serenity, and serenity can only be achieved by the avoidance of anxiety, and to avoid anxiety you have to know who you are and what you’re doing.”

As for us, we aim to deliver strong, market-beating returns. We are patient, opportunistic, and behaviorally aware. We seek to prevent permanent impairments of capital, not volatility.

Impairment means you don’t make back losses. This usually results from deteriorating business fundamentals or overpaying for an asset. We seek to mitigate both risks. Further, we seek to mitigate the risk we underperform our benchmark.

To deliver on our objective of high, market beating returns, we will need to protect against impairments associated with secular bull market tops. Fortunately, value investing has proved protective after similar past peaks. Our identity is long-term, value investors. We compare market expectations to business fundamentals and intrinsic values. We seek to capitalize on disconnections between the two when we have a variant perception.

We believe AI is transformative. Not all transformations lead to excellent investment opportunities. Air travel changed the world, but airlines have been the poster children of terrible investments (we think some airlines are attractive currently).

We invested in Nvidia early last year because we believed the market miscategorized Nvidia as a hardware company with imminent earnings risk. We thought Nvidia’s software, networking and innovation cycle created a broader competitive advantage, and the opportunity was huge.

We still believe Nvidia is earning the bulk of AI economic returns (so far). It’s a fantastic company with great returns and growth at the center of an economic boom. In our view, it’s not expensive at 29x FY 2027 (calendar 2026) earnings, which are expected to grow at 40%+. Their products are still in high demand with shortages.

The enduring fear has been that demand will roll over at some point, creating an air pocket in earnings and the stock. It’s possible. We aren’t smart enough to predict when that might happen. But we aren’t there yet. Market expectations are clouded by that fear, while business fundamentals remain stellar – a disconnect we are happy to continue to exploit.

On the other hand, we’ve avoided the neoclouds. Demand growth has been astounding, but they are burning significant cash given the elevated capital intensity. JP Morgan (JPM) recently initiated on CoreWeave, the leader, with an overweight.

It projected 2029 free cash flow of $3-4B. With a $79B enterprise value, one needs to believe in significantly brighter prospects over some time horizon to justify the current price.

As Peter Lynch said during the height of the Tech Bubble, “That’s not to say there’s no such thing as an overvalued market, but there’s no point in worrying about it.”

“Mommy, are we there yet?” No dear, not yet. But we’re getting closer.

Stay tuned for more about our positioning and individual holdings in our Q3 portfolio recap.
